<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Harmonic analysis & Fourier theory</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Harmonic analysis & Fourier theory

Decompose a function into waves, watch what survives. From the Hilbert basis $\{e^{2\pi inx}\}$ on the circle to the self-dual Gaussian on $\mathbb{R}$, and onward to Schwartz distributions, the uncertainty principle, and Pontryagin duality on locally compact abelian groups.

blocks.html:
1. Fourier series on the circle

Let $\mathbb{T} = \mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$ be the circle of circumference $1$. The exponentials

$$e_n(x) := e^{2\pi i n x},\qquad n\in\mathbb{Z}$$

are continuous, periodic, and pairwise orthogonal in $L^2(\mathbb{T})$:

$$\langle e_m, e_n\rangle = \int_0^1 e^{2\pi i (m-n)x}\,dx = \delta_{mn}.$$

Stone–Weierstrass says trigonometric polynomials are dense in $C(\mathbb{T})$, hence in $L^2(\mathbb{T})$. So $\{e_n\}_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal Hilbert basis of $L^2(\mathbb{T})$, and every $f\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$ has the expansion

$$f(x) \;=\; \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} \hat f(n)\, e_n(x),\qquad \hat f(n) = \int_0^1 f(x)\, e^{-2\pi i n x}\,dx,$$

convergent in $L^2$. Pythagoras becomes Parseval / Plancherel:

$$\int_0^1 |f(x)|^2\,dx \;=\; \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} |\hat f(n)|^2.$$

Widget — sliding partial sums

Pick a function and watch the partial sum $S_N f = \sum_{|n|\le N} \hat f(n) e_n$ approach $f$. Pay attention to the Gibbs overshoot near jumps of the square wave — about $9\%$ even as $N\to\infty$.

Partial Fourier sum $S_N f$
slide $N$, switch target
N = 6
Smooth bumps converge fast (exponentially in $N$). Discontinuous square wave shows the $\sim 9\%$ Gibbs overshoot regardless of $N$.

Pointwise convergence is harder than $L^2$ convergence

For $f\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$, $S_N f \to f$ in $L^2$. The deeper question — pointwise a.e. — is the celebrated Carleson theorem (1966): for $f\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$, $S_N f(x) \to f(x)$ for almost every $x$. Hunt later extended this to $L^p$ for $p>1$. In sharp contrast Kolmogorov constructed an $f\in L^1(\mathbb{T})$ whose Fourier series diverges everywhere — the $L^1$ side of the dichotomy is genuinely worse.

Smoothness $\to$ decay. If $f\in C^k(\mathbb{T})$ then $\hat f(n) = O(|n|^{-k})$ — integration by parts $k$ times. Conversely, sufficiently fast decay of $\hat f(n)$ forces $f$ to have many derivatives. This is the prototype of the Schwartz duality we'll exploit on $\mathbb{R}$.

See also
  • Banach and Hilbert spaces
  • Lᵖ spaces

<script>
/* =============================== §1 Fourier-series widget =============================== */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#fs-svg'), Nslider = $('#fs-N'), Nout = $('#fs-N-out'),
        fnSel = $('#fs-fn'), note = $('#fs-note');
  const W = 620, H = 280, padX = 28, padY = 22;
  const x0 = padX, x1 = W - padX, midY = H/2;
  const ampPx = (H/2 - padY);

  const targets = {
    square:   x => x < 0.5 ? 1 : -1,
    triangle: x => 1 - 4*Math.abs(x - 0.5),
    sawtooth: x => 2*x - 1,
    bump:     x => Math.exp(-30*(x-0.5)*(x-0.5)) - 0.32465247
  };
  // Numerical Fourier coefficients for the chosen target on [0,1].
  // For symmetric/antisymmetric simple waves the closed forms exist,
  // but a single quadrature works uniformly for all targets in the demo.
  function coeffs(fn, N){
    const M = 4096;
    const re = new Array(2*N+1).fill(0), im = new Array(2*N+1).fill(0);
    for(let k=0;k<M;k++){
      const xk = (k+0.5)/M;
      const fk = fn(xk);
      for(let n=-N;n<=N;n++){
        const phase = 2*Math.PI*n*xk;
        re[n+N] += fk*Math.cos(phase)/M;
        im[n+N] += -fk*Math.sin(phase)/M;
      }
    }
    return {re, im};
  }
  function partial(c, N, x){
    let s = 0;
    for(let n=-N;n<=N;n++){
      const phase = 2*Math.PI*n*x;
      s += c.re[n+N]*Math.cos(phase) - c.im[n+N]*Math.sin(phase);
    }
    return s;
  }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const N = parseInt(Nslider.value, 10);
    const fnName = fnSel.value;
    const fn = targets[fnName];
    Nout.textContent = `N = ${N}`;
    // axes
    const ax = SVG('line',{x1:x0,y1:midY,x2:x1,y2:midY,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1});
    const ay = SVG('line',{x1:x0,y1:padY,x2:x0,y2:H-padY,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1});
    svg.appendChild(ax); svg.appendChild(ay);
    // x labels
    [0,0.5,1].forEach(xx => {
      const tx = SVG('text',{x:x0+(x1-x0)*xx, y:midY+16, 'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)','text-anchor':'middle'});
      tx.textContent = xx === 0 ? '0' : (xx === 0.5 ? '½' : '1');
      svg.appendChild(tx);
    });

    // target (dashed pink)
    const Mp = 600;
    let dT = '';
    for(let i=0;i<=Mp;i++){
      const xi = i/Mp;
      const yi = fn(xi);
      const px = x0 + (x1-x0)*xi;
      const py = midY - ampPx*yi*0.85;
      dT += (i===0?'M':'L') + px.toFixed(1) + ' ' + py.toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dT, fill:'none', stroke:'var(--pink)', 'stroke-width':1.4,
      'stroke-dasharray':'4 3', opacity:'0.85'}));

    // partial sum (yellow)
    const c = coeffs(fn, N);
    let dS = '';
    for(let i=0;i<=Mp;i++){
      const xi = i/Mp;
      const yi = partial(c, N, xi);
      const px = x0 + (x1-x0)*xi;
      const py = midY - ampPx*yi*0.85;
      dS += (i===0?'M':'L') + px.toFixed(1) + ' ' + py.toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dS, fill:'none', stroke:'var(--yellow)', 'stroke-width':1.8}));

    // legend
    const lg1 = SVG('text',{x:x1-180,y:padY+12,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
    lg1.textContent = '— f(x)  (target, dashed)';
    const lg2 = SVG('text',{x:x1-180,y:padY+30,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--yellow)'});
    lg2.textContent = `— S_${N} f(x)  (partial sum)`;
    svg.appendChild(lg1); svg.appendChild(lg2);

    // contextual note
    if(fnName === 'square'){
      note.textContent = `Square wave at N = ${N}: Gibbs overshoot near the jumps stays at ~9% even as N grows. Mean-square error → 0; pointwise at the jump = mean of the two limits.`;
    } else if(fnName === 'triangle'){
      note.textContent = `Triangle wave at N = ${N}: continuous & piecewise-linear, so |hat f(n)| = O(1/n²). Convergence is uniform.`;
    } else if(fnName === 'sawtooth'){
      note.textContent = `Sawtooth at N = ${N}: jumps once per period, |hat f(n)| = O(1/n). Pointwise convergence to mean at the jump (Dirichlet).`;
    } else {
      note.textContent = `Smooth bump at N = ${N}: f ∈ C^∞ ⇒ |hat f(n)| decays faster than any polynomial. Convergence is exponentially fast.`;
    }
  }
  Nslider.addEventListener('input', render);
  fnSel.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Fourier transform on $\mathbb{R}$

On the line we can no longer index by $\mathbb{Z}$ — the dual variable $\xi$ is itself continuous. With our chosen normalization (no extra $2\pi$ factors floating around), define

$$\hat f(\xi) \;:=\; \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x)\, e^{-2\pi i \xi x}\,dx,\qquad f\in L^1(\mathbb{R}).$$

This integral converges absolutely; by dominated convergence, $\hat f$ is continuous. The Riemann–Lebesgue lemma adds that $\hat f(\xi)\to 0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$. So the transform sends $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ continuously into $C_0(\mathbb{R})$, with $\|\hat f\|_\infty \le \|f\|_1$. (The image is dense but not all of $C_0$; surjectivity fails for subtle reasons.)

The big four properties

operation in $x$operation in $\xi$
$\tau_a f(x) = f(x-a)$ (translation)$e^{-2\pi i a \xi}\,\hat f(\xi)$ (modulation)
$e^{2\pi i b x} f(x)$ (modulation)$\hat f(\xi - b)$ (translation)
$f(\lambda x)$, $\lambda > 0$ (dilation)$\lambda^{-1}\hat f(\xi/\lambda)$
$\overline{f(x)}$$\overline{\hat f(-\xi)}$
$f'(x)$$2\pi i \xi\,\hat f(\xi)$
$-2\pi i x f(x)$$\hat f'(\xi)$

The "differentiation $\leftrightarrow$ multiplication by polynomial" interchange is the engine that turns linear constant-coefficient PDEs into algebraic equations on the Fourier side.

Inversion and Plancherel

If $f$ and $\hat f$ are both in $L^1$, the inversion formula

$$f(x) \;=\; \int_{\mathbb{R}} \hat f(\xi)\, e^{2\pi i \xi x}\,d\xi$$

recovers $f$ from its transform. The harder content is Plancherel: although the Fourier transform is initially defined only on $L^1$, it extends uniquely to a unitary isomorphism

$$\mathcal{F}\colon L^2(\mathbb{R}) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L^2(\mathbb{R}),\qquad \|\hat f\|_2 = \|f\|_2.$$

The route is to first prove this on $L^1\cap L^2$ — where both definitions agree — then density-extend. With Plancherel, $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ becomes the natural home for spectral analysis.

Widget — Fourier transform of a tent

The triangular tent $\Lambda(x) = \max(1-|x|, 0)$ has $\hat\Lambda(\xi) = \mathrm{sinc}^2(\xi)$, with $\mathrm{sinc}(\xi) = \sin(\pi\xi)/(\pi\xi)$. The widget overlays the tent and its transform; you can pull the width with a slider and watch the $\xi$-side widen and narrow inversely.

$f$ vs $\hat f$ for a scaled tent
drag width: narrower $f$ ⇒ wider $\hat f$
a = 1.00
Tent of half-width a (top) ↔ a · sinc²(a ξ) (bottom). Squeezing the tent stretches the transform — Heisenberg in miniature.

/* =============================== §2 Fourier-transform widget =============================== */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#ft-svg'), wSl = $('#ft-w'), wOut = $('#ft-w-out'), note = $('#ft-note');
  const W=620, H=320, midX=W/2;
  const yTop = 50, yMid1 = 130;     // panel 1: f
  const yMid2 = 200, yBot = 280;    // panel 2: hat f
  const xPadL = 28, xPadR = 28;
  const xRange = 4;                 // domain in either direction (for both panels)

  function xToPx(x){ return midX + (x/xRange) * (W/2 - xPadR); }
  function clearAndDrawAxes(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    // panel 1 axis (f)
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xPadL, y1:yMid1, x2:W-xPadR, y2:yMid1, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:midX, y1:yTop, x2:midX, y2:yMid1+8, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // panel 2 axis (hat f)
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xPadL, y1:yBot, x2:W-xPadR, y2:yBot, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:midX, y1:yMid2, x2:midX, y2:yBot+8, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // labels
    const l1 = SVG('text',{x:xPadL+4, y:yTop+12,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'}); l1.textContent='f(x) — tent';
    svg.appendChild(l1);
    const l2 = SVG('text',{x:xPadL+4, y:yMid2+12,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'}); l2.textContent='hat f(ξ) — sinc²';
    svg.appendChild(l2);
    // x-axis tick labels at -2 and 2
    [-2, 2].forEach(xx => {
      const t = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(xx), y:yMid1+16,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)','text-anchor':'middle'});
      t.textContent = String(xx);
      svg.appendChild(t);
      const t2 = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(xx), y:yBot+16,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)','text-anchor':'middle'});
      t2.textContent = String(xx);
      svg.appendChild(t2);
    });
  }

  function plot(yFn, yMin, yMax, color, panelTop, panelBot){
    // sample x in [-xRange, xRange], project to pixel
    const N = 600;
    const ampPx = (panelBot - panelTop) * 0.85;
    let d = '';
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const xi = -xRange + 2*xRange*(i/N);
      const yi = yFn(xi);
      const py = panelBot - ampPx * (yi - yMin)/(yMax - yMin);
      const px = xToPx(xi);
      d += (i===0?'M':'L') + px.toFixed(1) + ' ' + py.toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d, fill:'none', stroke:color, 'stroke-width':1.8}));
  }

  function tent(a){
    return x => Math.max(0, 1 - Math.abs(x)/a);
  }
  function sinc2(a){
    // F[Λ(x/a)·a] (continuous version) = a · sinc²(a ξ); we plot a · sinc²(a ξ)
    return xi => {
      const u = a*xi;
      if(Math.abs(u) < 1e-9) return a;
      const s = Math.sin(Math.PI*u)/(Math.PI*u);
      return a * s * s;
    };
  }

  function render(){
    clearAndDrawAxes();
    const a = parseFloat(wSl.value);
    wOut.textContent = `a = ${a.toFixed(2)}`;
    // top panel: tent goes from 0 to 1 in y
    plot(tent(a), 0, 1.05, 'var(--yellow)', yTop, yMid1);
    // bottom panel: sinc² goes from a small negative to a (max at xi=0)
    plot(sinc2(a), -0.25, Math.max(1.05, 1.05*a), 'var(--cyan)', yMid2, yBot);
    note.textContent = `Width a = ${a.toFixed(2)}: tent supported on [-${a.toFixed(2)}, ${a.toFixed(2)}], transform peaks at hat f(0) = ${a.toFixed(2)} with first zeros at ξ = ±${(1/a).toFixed(3)}.`;
  }
  wSl.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

3. Convolution and the convolution theorem

Define the convolution of $f, g\in L^1(\mathbb{R})$ by

$$(f*g)(x) \;=\; \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(y)\, g(x-y)\,dy.$$

By Fubini–Tonelli the integral converges a.e. and $\|f*g\|_1 \le \|f\|_1\,\|g\|_1$ (Young's inequality at the corner $p=q=r=1$). Convolution is commutative, associative, and bilinear; equipped with $*$, $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ becomes a commutative Banach algebra.

The convolution theorem is the algebraic shadow of all this:

$$\widehat{f*g}(\xi) \;=\; \hat f(\xi)\,\hat g(\xi).$$

Proof: substitute and swap the order of integration (Fubini), then change variables $y\mapsto y$ and $x-y \mapsto z$. The dual identity $\widehat{fg} = \hat f * \hat g$ also holds (with appropriate hypotheses on $f, g$), so the Fourier transform swaps the two operations.

Why $L^1$ has no unit

If $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ had a unit $e$ for convolution, then $\hat e \cdot \hat f = \hat f$ for every $f$, forcing $\hat e \equiv 1$. But $\hat e \in C_0$ (Riemann–Lebesgue), so $\hat e \equiv 1$ is impossible. The convolution unit is morally the Dirac delta $\delta$ — a tempered distribution, not an $L^1$ function. We approximate it via approximate identities: families $\varphi_\varepsilon(x) = \varepsilon^{-1}\varphi(x/\varepsilon)$ with $\int\varphi = 1$ supported in shrinking neighborhoods, satisfying $\varphi_\varepsilon * f \to f$ in $L^p$ (and pointwise a.e.) as $\varepsilon\to 0^+$.

Two faces of multiplication. On the function side, multiplication $fg$ is pointwise. On the Fourier side, multiplication $\hat f\,\hat g$ is also pointwise — but it corresponds to convolution on the function side. So "what is multiplication?" depends on which world you are in. The Banach algebra $(L^1, *)$ and the algebra $C_0$ under pointwise product are intertwined by $\mathcal{F}$.

<script>
/* =============================== §3 Convolution widget =============================== */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#conv-svg'), tSl = $('#conv-t'), tOut = $('#conv-t-out'), note = $('#conv-note');
  const W=620, H=320, midX=W/2;
  const xRange = 4;
  function xToPx(x){ return midX + (x/xRange) * (W/2 - 28); }

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = parseFloat(tSl.value);
    tOut.textContent = `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}`;
    const yTopBase = 100, yTopHi = 60;
    const yBotBase = 280, yBotHi = 170;

    // panel 1 axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:24, y1:yTopBase, x2:W-24, y2:yTopBase, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // panel 2 axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:24, y1:yBotBase, x2:W-24, y2:yBotBase, stroke:'var(--line)'}));

    // tick labels
    [-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3].forEach(xx => {
      const t1 = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(xx), y:yTopBase+14,'font-size':10,fill:'var(--mute)','text-anchor':'middle'});
      t1.textContent = String(xx); svg.appendChild(t1);
      const t2 = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(xx), y:yBotBase+14,'font-size':10,fill:'var(--mute)','text-anchor':'middle'});
      t2.textContent = String(xx); svg.appendChild(t2);
    });

    // overlap region: max(-1, t-1) <= x <= min(1, t+1)
    const a = Math.max(-1, t-1), b = Math.min(1, t+1);
    if(b > a){
      const x1 = xToPx(a), x2 = xToPx(b);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:x1, y:yTopHi, width:x2-x1, height:yTopBase-yTopHi,
        fill:'var(--violet)', opacity:'0.32'}));
    }

    // f = 1 on [-1,1] in cyan
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:xToPx(-1), y:yTopHi, width:xToPx(1)-xToPx(-1), height:yTopBase-yTopHi,
      fill:'none', stroke:'var(--cyan)', 'stroke-width':2}));
    // g shifted by t = 1 on [t-1, t+1] in yellow
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:xToPx(t-1), y:yTopHi, width:xToPx(t+1)-xToPx(t-1), height:yTopBase-yTopHi,
      fill:'none', stroke:'var(--yellow)', 'stroke-width':2}));

    // labels for f, g(.-t)
    const lblF = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(-1)+6, y:yTopHi-6,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
    lblF.textContent='f'; svg.appendChild(lblF);
    const lblG = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(t+1)-6, y:yTopHi-6,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--yellow)','text-anchor':'end'});
    lblG.textContent='g(·−t)'; svg.appendChild(lblG);

    // bottom: tent  (f*g)(t) = max(0, 2 - |t|) on |t|<=2
    let d = '';
    const ampPx = yBotBase - yBotHi;
    for(let i=0;i<=400;i++){
      const ti = -3 + 6*(i/400);
      const yi = Math.max(0, 2 - Math.abs(ti));
      const py = yBotBase - ampPx * (yi/2.0);
      const px = xToPx(ti);
      d += (i===0?'M':'L') + px.toFixed(1) + ' ' + py.toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d, fill:'none', stroke:'var(--violet)', 'stroke-width':1.8}));

    // marker at current t
    const yT = Math.max(0, 2 - Math.abs(t));
    const py = yBotBase - ampPx * (yT/2.0);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:xToPx(t), cy:py, r:5, fill:'var(--violet)'}));
    const valLbl = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(t)+8, y:py-4,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--violet)'});
    valLbl.textContent = `(f*g)(${t.toFixed(2)}) = ${yT.toFixed(2)}`;
    svg.appendChild(valLbl);

    note.textContent = `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}: overlap interval = [${a.toFixed(2)}, ${b.toFixed(2)}], length = ${Math.max(0, b-a).toFixed(2)} = (f*g)(t).`;
  }
  tSl.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

4. Schwartz space and tempered distributions

The Fourier transform behaves cleanly on $L^1$ and $L^2$, but neither space is closed under both differentiation and multiplication by polynomials. We want a single space that is, and on which $\mathcal{F}$ is an automorphism. That space is Schwartz space:

$$\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^n) \;=\; \{f\in C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n) : \|f\|_{\alpha,\beta} := \sup_{x}|x^\alpha\,\partial^\beta f(x)| < \infty\quad\forall \alpha,\beta\}.$$

Smooth, with every derivative decaying faster than every polynomial. Examples: $e^{-\pi x^2}$ and any polynomial times a Gaussian; non-examples: $1/(1+x^2)$ (only $C^\infty$, but decays only like $x^{-2}$).

The relations

$$\widehat{x^\alpha f} = \tfrac{1}{(-2\pi i)^{|\alpha|}}\partial^\alpha \hat f,\qquad \widehat{\partial^\beta f} = (2\pi i\xi)^\beta \hat f$$

show $\mathcal{F}$ maps each seminorm $\|\cdot\|_{\alpha,\beta}$ continuously to $\|\cdot\|_{\beta,\alpha}$. Combined with the inversion formula on $\mathcal{S}$, this gives:

Theorem (Schwartz). $\mathcal{F}\colon \mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^n) \to \mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is a topological isomorphism with $\mathcal{F}^4 = \mathrm{id}$.

Tempered distributions

The tempered distributions $\mathcal{S}'(\mathbb{R}^n)$ are continuous linear functionals on $\mathcal{S}$. Every $f\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}$ of polynomial growth defines a tempered distribution via $\langle f,\varphi\rangle = \int f\varphi$, but $\mathcal{S}'$ contains much more:

$T\in\mathcal{S}'(\mathbb{R})$action on $\varphi$$\hat T$
$\delta$ (Dirac at $0$)$\varphi(0)$$\hat\delta = 1$ (constant function)
$1$ (constant)$\int\varphi$$\hat 1 = \delta$
$x$ (polynomial)$\int x\,\varphi$$\widehat{x} = \tfrac{1}{-2\pi i}\delta'$
$\mathrm{p.v.}\,1/x$$\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\varphi(x)/x\,dx$$-i\pi\,\mathrm{sgn}(\xi)$
$e^{2\pi i b x}$ (constant magnitude)$\int e^{2\pi i b x}\varphi(x)\,dx$$\delta_{b}$

The Fourier transform extends to $\mathcal{S}'$ by duality:

$$\langle \hat T, \varphi\rangle \;:=\; \langle T, \hat\varphi\rangle.$$

This is the right setting for the inversion formula on $L^2$ (where $f$ may not be in $L^1$), for $\widehat{\partial^\alpha f} = (2\pi i \xi)^\alpha \hat f$ to hold for tempered distributions, and for Sobolev spaces $H^s = \{f\in\mathcal{S}': (1+|\xi|^2)^{s/2}\hat f \in L^2\}$ to make sense for any real $s$.

5. Heisenberg uncertainty

You cannot simultaneously concentrate $f$ and $\hat f$. Any attempt to localize one spreads the other.

Heisenberg's inequality. For $f\in\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})$ with $\|f\|_2 = 1$, $$\Big(\int x^2|f(x)|^2\,dx\Big)^{1/2}\;\Big(\int \xi^2|\hat f(\xi)|^2\,d\xi\Big)^{1/2}\;\ge\;\frac{1}{4\pi}.$$ Equality iff $f$ is a Gaussian $f(x) = c\,e^{-\pi a x^2}$ for some $a>0$.

The proof is a one-line application of Cauchy–Schwarz to the operator identity

$$\left[\frac{d}{dx},\, x\right] = 1,\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad \tfrac{d}{dx}(xf) - x f' = f,$$

integrated against $\bar f$ and combined with $\widehat{f'} = 2\pi i \xi \hat f$ and Plancherel. The constant $1/(4\pi)$ comes out of the $2\pi$ in the Fourier exponent — change conventions and you change the constant, but the inequality and its physical content stand.

Reading: position / momentum

Think of $|f(x)|^2$ as a position density and $|\hat f(\xi)|^2$ (which is also a probability density, by Plancherel and $\|f\|_2 = 1$) as a "momentum" density. Their standard deviations $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_\xi$ satisfy

$$\sigma_x\,\sigma_\xi \;\ge\; \frac{1}{4\pi}.$$

Heisenberg's $\hbar/2$ in physics is the same statement under the convention $\hat f(p) = \int f(x) e^{-ipx/\hbar}\,dx/\sqrt{2\pi\hbar}$.

Widget — Gaussians saturate the bound

For the family $f_a(x) = (2a)^{1/4} e^{-\pi a x^2}$ (so $\|f_a\|_2 = 1$), the variances are explicit and the product is exactly $1/(4\pi)$.

Gaussian family $f_a$
slide $a$: $f$ narrows ⇒ $\hat f$ widens
a = 1.00
σ_x = …, σ_ξ = …, product = …
σ_x · σ_ξ = 1/(4π) ≈ 0.0796 for every a > 0. Gaussians live exactly on the Heisenberg bound; nothing else does.

6. Poisson summation

One identity binds Fourier series and the Fourier transform on $\mathbb{R}$ — and binds the function on $\mathbb{R}$ to its periodization on $\mathbb{T}$.

Poisson summation. For Schwartz $f\in\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})$, $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} f(n) \;=\; \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} \hat f(n).$$ More generally, $\sum_n f(x + n) = \sum_n \hat f(n)\,e^{2\pi i n x}$.

Proof sketch: define $F(x) = \sum_n f(x+n)$ on $\mathbb{T}$. Schwartz decay makes the sum and all its derivatives uniformly convergent, so $F\in C^\infty(\mathbb{T})$. Compute its Fourier coefficient:

$$\hat F(k) = \int_0^1 F(x) e^{-2\pi i k x}\,dx = \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x) e^{-2\pi i k x}\,dx = \hat f(k).$$

The Fourier expansion $F(x) = \sum_k \hat F(k) e^{2\pi i k x} = \sum_k \hat f(k) e^{2\pi i k x}$ is the second identity above; setting $x=0$ gives the first.

The theta function and $\zeta(s)$

Apply Poisson summation to $f_t(x) = e^{-\pi t x^2}$, which has $\hat f_t(\xi) = t^{-1/2} e^{-\pi \xi^2/t}$. The result is the modular transformation of Jacobi's theta function

$$\theta(t) := \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} e^{-\pi n^2 t},\qquad \theta(1/t) \;=\; \sqrt t\, \theta(t),\quad t > 0.$$

Riemann's 1859 derivation of the functional equation $\xi(s) = \xi(1-s)$ for $\xi(s) = \pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$ uses exactly this. Poisson summation is the analytic seed of all such functional equations — see Dirichlet series & Euler products for the L-function story.

Sampling theorem

If $\hat f$ is supported in $[-W, W]$ (band-limited), Poisson summation on the dilated $f(x/2W)$ recovers the Shannon–Nyquist sampling theorem: $f$ is determined by its values on the lattice $\frac{1}{2W}\mathbb{Z}$, with explicit reconstruction

$$f(x) = \sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} f\!\left(\frac{n}{2W}\right) \mathrm{sinc}\big(2W x - n\big).$$

Lower the sampling rate and you get aliasing — the high frequencies fold back onto the lattice in exactly the Poisson-summation pattern.

7. Heat kernel and the Gaussian eigenfunction

The Gaussian $g(x) = e^{-\pi x^2}$ is the most important function in Fourier analysis. It satisfies

$$\hat g \;=\; g.$$

The Gaussian is its own Fourier transform: an eigenfunction of $\mathcal{F}$ with eigenvalue $1$. (Proof: $g$ solves $g' + 2\pi x g = 0$; differentiating $\hat g$ via $\widehat{xf}$ gives $\hat g$ solving the same ODE, and matching values at $0$.) Together with translates, dilates, and Hermite polynomials, this seeds an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ on which $\mathcal{F}$ has eigenvalues $\{1, -i, -1, i\}$ — and thus $\mathcal{F}^4 = \mathrm{id}$ globally on $L^2$.

Heat equation as multiplication on the Fourier side

Consider the heat equation on $\mathbb{R}$ with initial datum $u(0,\cdot) = f$:

$$\partial_t u \;=\; \partial_x^2 u,\qquad x\in\mathbb{R},\ t>0.$$

Apply $\mathcal{F}$ in $x$:

$$\partial_t \hat u(t,\xi) \;=\; -4\pi^2 \xi^2\, \hat u(t, \xi),\qquad \hat u(0,\xi) = \hat f(\xi).$$

This is a pointwise ODE in $\xi$. Solution: $\hat u(t,\xi) = e^{-4\pi^2 t \xi^2}\,\hat f(\xi)$. Inverting:

$$u(t, x) \;=\; (K_t * f)(x),\qquad K_t(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi t}}\, e^{-x^2/(4t)}.$$

$K_t$ is the heat kernel: a Gaussian of standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{2t}$, with mass $1$ for every $t>0$ and $K_t \to \delta$ as $t \to 0^+$. So the heat semigroup $e^{t\partial_x^2}$ is convolution with $K_t$, which on the Fourier side is multiplication by $e^{-4\pi^2 t \xi^2}$ — exponential damping of high frequencies.

Probability connection. The same kernel $K_t$ is the density of standard Brownian motion at time $2t$. Convolution with $K_t$ in PDE land is taking expectation over a Brownian step in probability land — see probability theory for the random-walk picture.

Widget — heat-equation evolution

Pick an initial profile and watch the solution at times $t = 0.05, 0.2, 1$. The high-frequency wiggles damp first; the bell broadens.

$u(t, x) = (K_t * f)(x)$
slide $t$ to evolve
t = 0.10
u(t, x) = K_t * f. As t grows, fine features wash out — high frequencies die first.

<script>
/* =============================== §7 Heat-kernel widget =============================== */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#heat-svg'), tSl = $('#heat-t'), tOut = $('#heat-t-out'),
        fnSel = $('#heat-fn'), note = $('#heat-note');
  const W=620, H=280, midX=W/2, padY=18;
  const xRange = 5;
  function xToPx(x){ return midX + (x/xRange) * (W/2 - 24); }

  const initials = {
    bump: x => Math.exp(-(x*x)*4),
    two:  x => Math.exp(-((x-1.2)*(x-1.2))*8) + 0.7*Math.exp(-((x+1.5)*(x+1.5))*6),
    step: x => 0.5 + 0.5*Math.tanh(6*x),
  };

  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = parseFloat(tSl.value);
    tOut.textContent = `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}`;
    const fn = initials[fnSel.value];

    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:24, y1:H-padY, x2:W-24, y2:H-padY, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    [-4,-2,0,2,4].forEach(xx => {
      const tx = SVG('text',{x:xToPx(xx), y:H-padY+14,'font-size':10,fill:'var(--mute)','text-anchor':'middle'});
      tx.textContent = String(xx); svg.appendChild(tx);
    });

    // sample u(t,·) by direct convolution: u(t,x) = ∫ K_t(y) f(x-y) dy via discretization
    const Mp = 240;       // plot resolution
    const Mc = 600;       // convolution quadrature resolution
    const yMin = 0, yMax = 1.2;
    const ampPx = (H-padY) - 30;

    // ranges and step sizes
    const dy = 12.0/Mc;   // integrate y over [-6, 6]
    const sigma2 = Math.max(t*4, 1e-7);   // since u_t = u_xx → variance = 2t in our convention
                                          // actually with our PDE u_t = u_xx (no 4π factor) the
                                          // kernel is K_t(x) = 1/√(4πt) exp(-x²/(4t)).

    function uAt(x){
      if(t < 1e-6) return fn(x);
      let s = 0;
      const C = 1.0/Math.sqrt(4*Math.PI*t);
      for(let i=0;i<Mc;i++){
        const yi = -6 + dy*(i+0.5);
        s += C * Math.exp(-yi*yi/(4*t)) * fn(x-yi);
      }
      return s*dy;
    }

    let dInit='', dCur='';
    for(let i=0;i<=Mp;i++){
      const xi = -xRange + 2*xRange*(i/Mp);
      const yiInit = fn(xi);
      const yiCur  = uAt(xi);
      const px = xToPx(xi);
      const pyI = (H-padY) - ampPx*Math.max(0, Math.min(yiInit, yMax))/yMax;
      const pyC = (H-padY) - ampPx*Math.max(0, Math.min(yiCur,  yMax))/yMax;
      dInit += (i===0?'M':'L') + px.toFixed(1) + ' ' + pyI.toFixed(1) + ' ';
      dCur  += (i===0?'M':'L') + px.toFixed(1) + ' ' + pyC.toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    // initial dashed
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dInit, fill:'none', stroke:'var(--mute)', 'stroke-width':1.2,
      'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
    // current solid
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dCur,  fill:'none', stroke:'var(--cyan)', 'stroke-width':2}));

    // legend
    const lg1 = SVG('text',{x:W-200,y:padY+10,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    lg1.textContent='— f(x)  (initial, dashed)'; svg.appendChild(lg1);
    const lg2 = SVG('text',{x:W-200,y:padY+28,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
    lg2.textContent=`— u(t, x), t = ${t.toFixed(2)}`; svg.appendChild(lg2);

    if(t < 1e-6){
      note.textContent = 'At t = 0: u = f. Sliding t > 0 convolves with the heat kernel.';
    } else {
      note.textContent = `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}: kernel std deviation σ = √(2t) = ${Math.sqrt(2*t).toFixed(3)}. High frequencies damp by e^{-4π² t ξ²}.`;
    }
  }
  tSl.addEventListener('input', render);
  fnSel.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

8. Pontryagin duality (teaser)

Fourier series on $\mathbb{T}$ and the Fourier transform on $\mathbb{R}$ are not two theorems — they are one theorem in two key signatures. The unifying frame is Pontryagin duality on locally compact abelian (LCA) groups.

For $G$ an LCA group, the dual group is

$$\widehat G \;=\; \Hom_{\mathrm{cts}}(G,\, U(1)) \;=\; \{\text{continuous group homomorphisms } \chi\colon G\to U(1)\}.$$

Endow $\widehat G$ with the compact-open topology. It is also LCA, and the canonical map $G\to\widehat{\widehat G}$, $g\mapsto (\chi\mapsto \chi(g))$, is a topological group isomorphism. The classical Fourier theorems are special cases:

$G$$\widehat G$"Fourier transform"
$\mathbb{R}$$\mathbb{R}$ (self-dual via $\chi_\xi(x) = e^{2\pi i \xi x}$)continuous Fourier transform
$\mathbb{T} = \mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$$\mathbb{Z}$ (via $\chi_n(x) = e^{2\pi i n x}$)Fourier series: $\hat f \in \ell^2(\mathbb{Z})$
$\mathbb{Z}$$\mathbb{T}$discrete-time Fourier transform
$\mathbb{Z}/N$$\mathbb{Z}/N$ (self-dual finite)discrete Fourier transform (DFT)
$\mathbb{Q}_p$$\mathbb{Q}_p$ (self-dual via additive characters)$p$-adic Fourier analysis
$\mathbb{A}_K = \prod'_v K_v$ (adèles)$\mathbb{A}_K$ (self-dual)adelic harmonic analysis (Tate's thesis)

The Plancherel theorem says: with appropriately compatible Haar measures on $G$ and $\widehat G$, the Fourier transform $\mathcal{F}\colon L^1(G)\cap L^2(G) \to L^2(\widehat G)$ extends to a unitary isomorphism $L^2(G)\xrightarrow{\sim} L^2(\widehat G)$. Setting $G = \mathbb{R}$ recovers classical Plancherel; $G = \mathbb{T}$ recovers Parseval; $G = \mathbb{Z}/N$ is unitarity of the DFT matrix divided by $\sqrt N$.

Looking ahead. Tate (1950) used Pontryagin duality on the adèles $\mathbb{A}_K$ to give a unified, conceptual proof of the analytic continuation and functional equation of Hecke L-functions — the same Poisson-summation idea (§6) lifted to LCA groups. This is the bridge from harmonic analysis to modern automorphic forms.
